2604.11125.title: A Proposed Biomedical Data Policy Framework to Reduce Fragmentation, Improve Quality, and Incentivize Sharing in Indian Healthcare in the era of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Health
2604.11125.paper_summary: India generates vast biomedical data through postgraduate research, government hospital services and audits, government schemes, private hospitals and their electronic medical record (EMR) systems, insurance programs and standalone clinics. Unfortunately, these resources remain fragmented across institutional silos and vendor-locked EMR systems. The fundamental bottleneck is not technological but economic and academic. There is a systemic misalignment of incentives that renders data sharing a high-risk, low-reward activity for individual researchers and institutions. Until India's academic promotion criteria, institutional rankings, and funding mechanisms explicitly recognize and reward data curation as professional work, the nation's AI ambitions will remain constrained by fragmented, non-interoperable datasets. We propose a multi-layered incentive architecture integrating recognition of data papers in National Medical Commission (NMC) promotion criteria, incorporation of open data metrics into the National Institutional Ranking Framework (NIRF), adoption of Shapley Value-based revenue sharing in federated learning consortia, and establishment of institutional data stewardship as a mainstream professional role. Critical barriers to data sharing, including fear of data quality scrutiny, concerns about misinterpretation, and selective reporting bias, are addressed through mandatory data quality assessment, structured peer review, and academic credit for auditing roles. The proposed framework directly addresses regulatory constraints introduced by the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA), while constructively engaging with the National Data Sharing and Accessibility Policy (NDSAP), Biotech-PRIDE Guidelines, and the Anusandhan National Research Foundation (ANRF) guidelines.
2604.11125.paper_summary_zh: 印度透過研究生研究、政府醫院服務和審計、政府計劃、私立醫院及其電子病歷（EMR）系統、保險計劃和獨立診所產生了大量生物醫學數據。<lb/>不幸的是，這些資源仍然分散在各個機構的孤島和供應商鎖定的EMR系統中。<lb/>根本的瓶頸不是技術性問題，而是經濟和學術問題。<lb/>存在一種系統性的激勵不對齊，使得數據共享對於個別研究者和機構來說成為一項高風險、低回報的活動。<lb/>在印度的學術晉升標準、機構排名和資金機制明確承認並獎勵數據管理作為專業工作之前，該國的人工智慧雄心將受到分散的、不可互操作數據集的限制。<lb/>我們提出了一個多層次的激勵架構，整合了在國家醫療委員會（NMC）晉升標準中對數據論文的認可、將開放數據指標納入國家機構排名框架（NIRF）、在聯邦學習聯盟中採用基於Shapley值的收益分享，以及將機構數據管理建立為主流專業角色。<lb/>針對數據共享的關鍵障礙，包括對數據質量審查的恐懼、對誤解的擔憂以及選擇性報告偏見，通過強制數據質量評估、結構化同行評審和審計角色的學術認可來解決。<lb/>所提出的框架直接解決了2023年數位個人數據保護法（DPDPA）引入的監管限制，同時與國家數據共享與可及性政策（NDSAP）、生物技術-PRIDE指導方針和Anusandhan國家研究基金會（ANRF）指導方針進行建設性互動。
2604.11125.author: Nikhil Mehta et.al.
2604.11125.authors: Nikhil Mehta, Sachin Gupta, Gouri RP Anand
